abstract: Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) are a new family of evolutionary variation operators that replace the fixed mutation, crossover, and hand-designed heuristics of classical evolutionary search with autonomous coding agents. Rather than confining a language model to candidate generation within a prescribed pipeline, AVO instantiates variation as a self-directed agent loop that can consult the current lineage, a domain-specific knowledge base, and execution feedback to propose, repair, critique, and verify implementation edits. We evaluate AVO on attention, among the most aggressively optimized kernel targets in AI, on NVIDIA Blackwell (B200) GPUs. Over 7 days of continuous autonomous evolution on multi-head attention, AVO discovers kernels that outperform cuDNN by up to 3.5% and FlashAttention-4 by up to 10.5% across the evaluated configurations. The discovered optimizations transfer readily to grouped-query attention, requiring only 30 minutes of additional autonomous adaptation and yielding gains of up to 7.0% over cuDNN and 9.3% over FlashAttention-4. Together, these results show that agentic variation operators move beyond prior LLM-in-the-loop evolutionary pipelines by elevating the agent from candidate generator to variation operator, and can discover performance-critical micro-architectural optimizations that produce kernels surpassing state-of-the-art expert-engineered attention implementations on today's most advanced GPU hardware.

citations.fullCitation: Bernardino Romera-Paredes, Mohammadamin Barekatain, Alexander Novikov, Matej Balog, M. Pawan Kumar, Emilien Dupont, Francisco J. R. Ruiz, Jordan S. Ellenberg, Pengming Wang, Omar Fawzi, Pushmeet Kohli, and Alhussein Fawzi. Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models. Nature, 625:468–475, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper introduces FunSearch, a flagship system for evolutionary search using large language models. The AVO paper frequently cites and contrasts its agentic approach with FunSearch's 'LLM-in-the-loop' pipeline, where the LLM is confined to a fixed candidate generation step, making this a foundational work that highlights the limitations AVO aims to overcome.
citations.title: Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models

citations.fullCitation: Alexander Novikov, Ngân V˜u, Marvin Eisenberger, Emilien Dupont, Po-Sen Huang, Adam Zsolt Wagner, Sergey Shirobokov, Borislav Kozlovskii, Francisco J. R. Ruiz, Abbas Mehrabian, M. Pawan Kumar, Abigail See, Swarat Chaudhuri, George Holland, Alex Davies, Sebastian Nowozin, Pushmeet Kohli, and Matej Balog. Alphaevolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery, 2025. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.13131.
citations.justification: Similar to FunSearch, AlphaEvolve is presented as a key example of prior art in LLM-augmented evolutionary search. The AVO paper explicitly uses it to illustrate the standard, rigid framework of sampling, generation, and evaluation that its own autonomous agent-based variation operator replaces, making it a critical point of comparison for AVO's novel methodology.
citations.title: Alphaevolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery

citations.fullCitation: Tri Dao, Daniel Y. Fu, Stefano Ermon, Atri Rudra, and Christopher Ré. Flashattention: Fast and memory-efficient exact attention with io-awareness. 2022. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.14135.
citations.justification: This paper introduced the highly efficient, I/O-aware attention algorithm that became the standard for high-performance implementations. It establishes the fundamental problem domain and algorithmic context for the AVO paper's experiments, as AVO's goal is to autonomously discover further optimizations on top of this already advanced foundation.
citations.title: Flashattention: Fast and memory-efficient exact attention with io-awareness

citations.fullCitation: Ted Zadouri, Markus Hoehnerbach, Jay Shah, Timmy Liu, Vijay Thakkar, and Tri Dao. Flashattention-4: Algorithm and kernel pipelining co-design for asymmetric hardware scaling. 2026. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.05451.
citations.justification: This paper presents the latest version of FlashAttention, specifically optimized for the NVIDIA Blackwell architecture used in the AVO study. It serves as the primary state-of-the-art performance baseline, and outperforming this expert-engineered kernel is the main empirical result used to demonstrate the effectiveness of the AVO approach.
citations.title: Flashattention-4: Algorithm and kernel pipelining co-design for asymmetric hardware scaling

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "AVO: Agentic Variation Operators for Autonomous Evolutionary Search."

---

## Detailed Report: Agentic Variation Operators for Autonomous Evolutionary Search

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a team of authors affiliated with NVIDIA.
The authors are: Terry Chen, Zhifan Ye, Bing Xu, Zihao Ye, Timmy Liu, Ali Hassani, Tianqi Chen, Andrew Kerr, Haicheng Wu, Yang Xu, Yu-Jung Chen, Hanfeng Chen, Aditya Kane, Ronny Krashinsky, Ming-Yu Liu, Vinod Grover, Luis Ceze, Roger Bringmann, John Tran, Wei Liu, Fung Xie, Michael Lightstone, and Humphrey Shi. Terry Chen, Zhifan Ye, and Bing Xu are noted as equal contributors.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The work presented in this paper builds upon and distinguishes itself from existing approaches in evolutionary search, particularly those leveraging large language models (LLMs). Traditional evolutionary search frameworks optimize over a population of candidate solutions by iteratively applying variation operators (mutation and crossover) and evaluation functions. Recent advancements have integrated LLMs into this process, replacing hand-crafted mutation operators with learned code generation capabilities.

Prior LLM-augmented evolutionary methods, such as FunSearch and AlphaEvolve, typically confine the LLM to the "Generate" step within a fixed algorithmic pipeline. In these systems, a framework, often heuristic-based, handles parent sampling, evaluation, and population management, while the LLM's role is primarily to produce a single output candidate based on selected parents. Even more advanced approaches, like LoongFlow, which uses a fixed "Plan-Execute-Summarize" pipeline, or TTT-Discover, which updates the LLM policy itself, still maintain a rigid algorithmic structure where the LLM's invocation is prescribed. This confinement limits the LLM's ability to engage in the iterative, multi-step engineering workflows often required for deep optimization. Specifically, the LLM cannot proactively consult reference materials, test its changes, interpret feedback, or revise its approach before committing a candidate solution.

This limitation becomes particularly pronounced in the context of aggressively optimized computational targets, such as GPU kernels for deep learning operations. For these targets, further performance improvements typically require sustained, iterative interaction with the development environment, including analysis of profiler output, hardware documentation consultation, implementation and testing of candidate optimizations, and systematic debugging based on accumulated experience. Expert-engineered implementations, like those in the FlashAttention lineage and NVIDIA’s cuDNN library, already push throughput close to hardware limits, necessitating a deep, iterative engineering approach for further gains.

The present work positions itself by leveraging recent progress in deep agents, which demonstrate that LLMs augmented with planning, persistent memory, and tool use can autonomously navigate complex multi-step engineering tasks. By elevating the LLM's role from a candidate generator within a fixed pipeline to an autonomous "Agentic Variation Operator" (AVO), this research aims to overcome the limitations of previous LLM-augmented evolutionary methods. AVO proposes a paradigm where the agent itself orchestrates the entire variation process, including sampling, generation, and evaluation, enabling a more adaptive and iterative search for micro-architectural optimizations. This approach represents a shift from LLM-in-the-loop candidate generation to an LLM-as-the-loop variation operator, providing the agent with full agency over its interaction with the environment and the accumulated knowledge.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to introduce and evaluate Agentic Variation Operators (AVO), a new family of evolutionary variation operators that fundamentally redefines the role of language models in evolutionary search. Instead of restricting LLMs to single-turn candidate generation within a pre-defined workflow, AVO aims to elevate them to autonomous coding agents capable of self-directed, iterative optimization.

The motivation for this objective stems from several key observations and challenges in the field of high-performance computing and AI-driven optimization:

1.  **Limitations of Existing LLM-augmented Evolutionary Search:** Current LLM-augmented evolutionary search systems confine the language model to a candidate generation step. This constrains the LLM's ability to engage in complex, multi-step engineering processes. The LLM typically produces a single output and lacks the capacity to consult external resources, perform iterative testing, interpret feedback, diagnose issues, or revise its strategy over an extended session. This rigid pipeline is insufficient for discovering the deep, iterative micro-architectural optimizations needed for state-of-the-art performance targets.

2.  **Demands of High-Performance Kernel Optimization:** The optimization of critical operations, such as attention kernels in Transformer architectures, on modern GPU hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Blackwell B200) is an arduous and time-consuming task. Implementations like FlashAttention-4 (FA4) and NVIDIA's cuDNN library represent the culmination of months of manual, expert engineering effort. Further improvements necessitate a sophisticated and iterative development cycle involving:
    *   Studying intricate hardware documentation.
    *   Analyzing detailed profiler outputs to pinpoint performance bottlenecks.
    *   Implementing and testing candidate optimizations.
    *   Diagnosing correctness failures and performance regressions.
    *   Continuously revising optimization strategies based on acquired experience and feedback.

3.  **Emergence of Deep Agents:** Recent advancements in deep agents, which integrate LLMs with planning capabilities, persistent memory, and tool use, have demonstrated their capacity to autonomously navigate complex, multi-step engineering workflows. These agents have shown promise in tasks ranging from resolving complex software issues to generating key deep learning software components. This progress motivates exploring a more autonomous role for LLMs in evolutionary search.

By addressing these motivations, the specific objectives of the research are to:
*   Develop a framework that allows an LLM-powered agent to act as the primary variation operator, autonomously deciding when to consult resources, what to edit, and when to evaluate outcomes.
*   Demonstrate the effectiveness of AVO by applying it to the challenging problem of optimizing multi-head attention (MHA) kernels on cutting-edge hardware (NVIDIA Blackwell B200 GPUs).
*   Quantitatively evaluate whether AVO can discover MHA kernels that surpass the performance of highly optimized, expert-engineered implementations like cuDNN and FlashAttention-4.
*   Assess the transferability of the agent-discovered optimizations by adapting the evolved MHA kernel to a related task, grouped-query attention (GQA), with minimal additional autonomous effort.
*   Provide an analysis of the types of micro-architectural optimizations discovered by the agent, illustrating its capacity for hardware-level reasoning rather than superficial code transformations.

In essence, the paper aims to validate the hypothesis that elevating the LLM to a self-directed, agentic variation operator can unlock a new level of autonomous discovery for performance-critical software, moving beyond mere candidate generation to genuine iterative engineering.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The core methodology of this research revolves around the introduction and implementation of Agentic Variation Operators (AVO), which fundamentally changes the role of LLMs in evolutionary search.

**4.1 Agentic Variation Operator (AVO) Formulation**

AVO replaces the traditional two-stage variation operator, `Vary(P_t) = Generate(Sample(P_t))`, with a single autonomous agent run: `Vary(P_t) = Agent(P_t, K, f)`. Here:
*   `P_t = {(x_1, f(x_1)), ..., (x_t, f(x_t))}` represents the full lineage of solutions discovered so far, including their scores. In this context, each `x_i` is a CUDA kernel implementation (source code, potentially with inline PTX).
*   `K` is a comprehensive domain-specific knowledge base, comprising CUDA programming guides, PTX ISA documentation, NVIDIA Blackwell architecture specifications, and source code of existing high-performance kernel implementations like FlashAttention-4.
*   `f` is the scoring function, which evaluates a candidate `x_i` along two dimensions: numerical correctness against a reference implementation and throughput in TFLOPS on the target hardware. A candidate failing correctness is assigned a zero score. The scoring function is n-dimensional, `f(x_i) = (f_1(x_i), ..., f_n(x_i))`, where `f_j` is the score for test configuration `j`.

For this study, AVO is instantiated in a single-lineage autonomous run, starting from a seed program `x_0` and iteratively producing improved versions `x_1, x_2, ..., x_t`. The accumulated lineage `P_t` serves as contextual information for subsequent variation steps.

**4.2 Anatomy of a Variation Step**

A single variation step, resulting in `x_{t+1}` from `P_t`, is an autonomous agent loop rather than a predefined sequence of operations. The agent employed is a general-purpose coding agent equipped with:
*   **Planning capabilities:** To strategize optimization directions.
*   **Tool use:** Access to standard software engineering tools such as autonomous code editing, shell command execution, file system navigation, and documentation retrieval.
*   **Persistent memory:** Maintained through its conversation history, which accumulates the context of prior edits, compiler outputs, profiling results, and reasoning across the entire evolutionary process.

Within a variation step, the agent performs multiple internal actions:
1.  **Consultation:** It frequently examines multiple prior implementations in `P_t`, comparing their profiling characteristics to identify bottlenecks and opportunities. It also consults `K` to understand relevant hardware constraints.
2.  **Implementation:** Proposes and applies code modifications.
3.  **Evaluation:** Invokes `f` to test the result for correctness and performance.
4.  **Diagnosis and Revision:** If a candidate fails correctness or does not improve performance, the agent diagnoses the issue and revises its approach. This edit-evaluate-diagnose cycle continues until a satisfactory `x_{t+1}` is committed.

New versions are committed and added to the persistent lineage `P_t` only if they pass correctness checks and match or improve the benchmark score relative to the best committed version. Unsuccessful intermediate attempts remain part of the agent's internal trajectory but are not added to the committed lineage.

**4.3 Continuous Evolution**

The AVO agent operates as a continuous loop, producing new solutions over extended periods without human intervention. Each committed version is stored as a git commit along with its score, ensuring full state continuity. To maintain progress during long-running optimization, AVO incorporates a self-supervision mechanism. This "Supervisor Agent" detects stagnation (when the agent exhausts an exploration line) or unproductive cycles. Upon detection, it intervenes by reviewing the overall evolutionary trajectory and steering the main agent towards new candidate optimization directions, providing fresh perspective.

**4.4 Experimental Setup**

*   **Agent:** An internally-developed general-purpose coding agent powered by frontier LLMs. No task-specific modifications were made beyond providing the domain-specific knowledge base `K` and scoring function `f`.
*   **Hardware and Software:** All experiments were conducted on NVIDIA B200 GPUs, utilizing CUDA 13.1 and PyTorch 2.10.0.
*   **Baselines:** Performance was compared against two state-of-the-art implementations:
    *   **cuDNN:** NVIDIA's closed-source attention kernel (version 9.19.1), specifically optimized for Blackwell.
    *   **FlashAttention-4 (FA4):** The latest open-source attention kernel optimized for Blackwell (commit 71bf77c).
*   **Benchmark Configurations:**
    *   **Multi-Head Attention (MHA):** Evaluated forward prefilling throughput for head dimension 128 and BF16 precision, with 16 heads. Sequence lengths varied `{4096, 8192, 16384, 32768}`, maintaining a fixed total of 32768 tokens by adjusting batch size. Both causal and non-causal masking were tested.
    *   **Grouped-Query Attention (GQA):** The evolved MHA kernel was adapted by the AVO agent to support GQA. Two configurations from Qwen3 models were used: 32 query heads with 4 KV heads (group size 8) and 32 query heads with 8 KV heads (group size 4), both with head dimension 128 and BF16 precision. Causal and non-causal masking were evaluated.
*   **Measurement Protocol:** Throughput was measured using the timing script from the FA4 repository, employing the same number of warm-up and repeat rounds as the FA4 paper. Each experiment was run 10 times to obtain average performance and standard deviation.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The study evaluated Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) on attention kernel optimization over a 7-day continuous autonomous evolution period, focusing on NVIDIA Blackwell (B200) GPUs.

**5.1 Multi-Head Attention (MHA) Performance**

*   **Causal Attention:** AVO consistently outperformed both baselines across all tested configurations (sequence lengths 4K, 8K, 16K, 32K).
    *   Gains over cuDNN ranged from +0.4% to +3.5%.
    *   Gains over FlashAttention-4 (FA4) ranged from +5.0% to +10.5%.
    *   The highest throughput achieved was 1668 TFLOPS at BF16 precision for a sequence length of 16K (bs=2).
*   **Non-Causal Attention:** AVO achieved modest performance gains at longer sequence lengths (e.g., +1.8% to +2.4% over cuDNN for sequence lengths greater than 16384). At shorter sequence lengths, AVO's performance was comparable to both baselines, falling within measurement noise. The maximum throughput observed was 1668 TFLOPS.

**5.2 Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) Transferability**

To assess the generalizability of the discovered optimizations, the AVO agent was prompted to adapt the evolved MHA kernel to support GQA. This adaptation was completed autonomously by the agent in approximately 30 minutes, without human guidance.

*   **Causal GQA (Group Size 8 and 4):** AVO outperformed both baselines across all configurations.
    *   Gains over cuDNN reached up to +7.0%.
    *   Gains over FA4 reached up to +9.3%.
*   **Non-Causal GQA (Group Size 8 and 4):** AVO also demonstrated performance improvements.
    *   Gains over cuDNN reached up to +6.0%.
    *   Gains over FA4 reached up to +4.5%.

These results indicate that the optimizations discovered during MHA evolution were not specific to the MHA configurations but generalized effectively to the distinct compute and memory access patterns of GQA.

**5.3 Evolution Trajectory**

Over the 7-day evolution, the AVO agent produced 40 committed kernel versions. This represented a subset of over 500 internal candidate optimization directions explored, which included attempts that failed correctness, regressed throughput, or were abandoned.

*   **Scale of Exploration:** The agent's ability to explore hundreds of optimization directions, each involving documentation review, implementation, compilation, testing, and profiling, significantly exceeds human capacity in the same timeframe.
*   **Discrete Jumps in Performance:** Throughput improvements were observed as distinct steps separated by plateaus. The five largest gains correlated with architectural changes, such as the introduction of QK-PV interleaving with bitmask causal masking (v8), a restructured single-pass softmax (v13), branchless accumulator rescaling (v20), correction/MMA pipeline overlap (v30), and register rebalancing (v33).
*   **Diminishing Returns:** Earlier versions (v1-v20) yielded the largest absolute gains, significantly closing the gap with baselines. Later versions (v21-v40) contributed smaller but cumulative improvements through cycle-level scheduling and resource allocation, reflecting a shift from coarse-grained to fine-grained micro-architectural tuning.

**5.4 Agent-Discovered Optimizations**

The analysis identified multi-level optimizations. Three representative examples illustrate the agent's hardware-level reasoning:

1.  **Branchless Accumulator Rescaling (v19 → v20):**
    *   **Bottleneck Identified:** A conditional branch for output accumulator rescaling in the online softmax algorithm introduced warp synchronization overhead and prevented the use of lighter memory fences.
    *   **Agent's Approach:** Replaced the conditional branch with a branchless speculative path, where the rescale factor is always computed, and a predicated select substitutes 1.0 when rescaling is unnecessary. This eliminated warp divergence, enabling the use of a non-blocking memory fence.
    *   **Impact:** +8.1% geomean throughput on non-causal attention and +1.6% on causal attention. The asymmetry is due to the branchless path applying only to fully unmasked iterations.

2.  **Correction/MMA Pipeline Overlap (v29 → v30):**
    *   **Bottleneck Identified:** In the dual Q-stage attention pipeline, the correction warp waited for both PV GEMMs to complete before starting normalization, leading to idle time during the second GEMM.
    *   **Agent's Approach:** Restructured the pipeline to allow the correction warp to begin normalizing the first stage's output immediately after its PV GEMM, overlapping this with the second stage's PV GEMM.
    *   **Impact:** +1.1% geomean throughput on non-causal attention and +0.4% on causal attention.

3.  **Register Rebalancing Across Warp Groups (v32 → v33):**
    *   **Bottleneck Identified:** Uneven register allocation resulted in correction warps spilling values to slower local memory due to a limited 80-register budget, while softmax warps had excess capacity.
    *   **Agent's Approach:** Redistributed 8 registers from the softmax group (which had low peak usage) to the correction and other groups, changing the allocation from 192/80/48 to 184/88/56. This reduced local memory spills in the critical path correction warps.
    *   **Impact:** +2.1% geomean throughput on non-causal attention and approximately 0% on causal attention.

These optimizations demonstrate the agent's capacity to reason jointly about multiple hardware subsystems, including synchronization, memory ordering, pipeline scheduling, and register allocation, highlighting a depth of autonomous hardware-level reasoning.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The research introduces Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) and demonstrates their capabilities, which has several significant implications and potential impacts:

1.  **Redefining Evolutionary Search with LLMs:** AVO fundamentally redefines the role of Large Language Models (LLMs) in evolutionary search. By elevating the LLM from a passive candidate generator within a fixed pipeline to an autonomous, self-directed variation operator, AVO enables the agent to actively plan, implement, test, diagnose, and revise its optimization strategies over extended sessions. This paradigm shift moves beyond superficial code transformations and allows for a more integrated and iterative engineering process, more akin to human expert development.

2.  **Discovery of State-of-the-Art Micro-Architectural Optimizations:** The most direct impact is the discovery of highly optimized attention kernels that surpass the performance of existing state-of-the-art, expert-engineered implementations. For multi-head attention (MHA) on NVIDIA Blackwell (B200) GPUs, AVO-generated kernels demonstrated performance gains of up to 3.5% over cuDNN and 10.5% over FlashAttention-4 across evaluated configurations. This achievement in a domain as aggressively optimized as attention kernels suggests a significant leap in autonomous code optimization capabilities.

3.  **Demonstrated Hardware-Level Reasoning:** The detailed analysis of agent-discovered optimizations (e.g., branchless accumulator rescaling, pipeline overlap, register rebalancing) illustrates that the agent is capable of reasoning about complex interactions between multiple hardware subsystems, including synchronization, memory ordering, instruction pipeline scheduling, and register allocation. This indicates that AVO can perform genuine hardware-level reasoning, rather than merely applying pre-defined patterns or superficial code changes. This depth of reasoning is crucial for squeezing out marginal gains on highly optimized hardware.

4.  **Transferability of Optimizations:** The successful and rapid autonomous adaptation of the evolved MHA kernel to grouped-query attention (GQA), yielding further performance gains, demonstrates the transferability and generalizability of the agent's discovered optimizations. This indicates that the knowledge acquired during one optimization task can be effectively leveraged for related, but distinct, computational patterns, enhancing the practical utility of the AVO approach.

5.  **Autonomous and Continuous Optimization:** The 7-day continuous autonomous evolution, involving over 500 internal exploration directions and 40 committed versions, highlights the scalability and endurance of the AVO framework. The self-supervision mechanism, which detects stagnation and redirects the search, is critical for enabling such long-running, unsupervised optimization processes. This capability can significantly reduce the human effort and time required for high-performance software development.

6.  **Broader Applicability:** While demonstrated on attention kernels, the principles of AVO are formulated at the level of general variation operators. This suggests a potential for broader application beyond this specific domain. AVO could be applied to other performance-critical software systems, diverse hardware platforms, and various engineering or scientific domains that demand sustained, iterative, and autonomous exploration for optimization. This work points towards a future where AI agents play a more central, autonomous role in discovering fundamental algorithmic and micro-architectural improvements across a wide range of computational challenges.
overview:
## Autonomous Optimization via Agentic Variation

Traditional evolutionary search frameworks for code optimization typically rely on a fixed, algorithmic pipeline. In these systems, a host framework handles task management—such as sampling parent solutions and evaluating new candidates—while a Large Language Model (LLM) is used as a constrained "generator" within a single-turn workflow. While effective for basic programming tasks, this rigid "LLM-in-the-loop" structure often fails to address the deep, iterative engineering required for performance-critical targets, such as GPU kernels. Optimizing for modern hardware like the NVIDIA Blackwell architecture necessitates a sustained cycle of documentation review, profiling, debugging, and micro-architectural refinement.

![Evolutionary Search Comparison](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.24517v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: Comparison between classical evolutionary search (EVO) and Agentic Variation Operators (AVO). While classical EVO uses LLMs in a single-turn or fixed workflow, AVO elevates the LLM to an autonomous agent capable of multi-step planning, tool use, and iterative debugging.*

Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) represent a shift toward an "LLM-as-the-loop" paradigm. Instead of being confined to a single generation step, the LLM is integrated into an autonomous agent equipped with persistent memory and professional engineering tools. This allows the agent to act as a self-directed variation operator that can explore hundreds of optimization directions, interpret hardware-specific profiler feedback, and autonomously revise its implementation before committing a candidate to the population. This method has demonstrated the ability to discover attention kernels that surpass the performance of highly optimized, expert-engineered libraries like cuDNN and FlashAttention-4 on the NVIDIA B200 GPU.

## The AVO Framework and Methodology

The fundamental difference in AVO lies in the mathematical formulation of the variation process. In traditional LLM-augmented evolutionary search, the variation step is typically defined as a single function call:

$$
Vary(P_t) = \text{Generate}(\text{Sample}(P_t))
$$

Here, the framework selects parents from the current population $P_t$ and asks the LLM to generate a descendant. In contrast, AVO defines the variation step as a complete autonomous agent run:

$$
Vary(P_t) = \text{Agent}(P_t, K, f) \to (x_{t+1}, f(x_{t+1}))
$$

In this formulation, $P_t$ is the accumulated lineage of solutions:

$$
P_t = \{(x_1, f(x_1)), \dots, (x_t, f(x_t))\}
$$

where each $x_i$ is a candidate implementation and $f(x_i)$ is its corresponding score. The agent also has access to a domain-specific knowledge base $K$, which contains hardware specifications (e.g., NVIDIA Blackwell PTX ISA) and reference codebases. The scoring function $f$ evaluates both the numerical correctness and the hardware throughput of a solution. The scoring is n-dimensional, $f(x_i) = (f_1(x_i), \dots, f_n(x_i))$, representing performance across different test configurations $j$.

![AVO Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.24517v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The AVO main agent loop and its interaction with the population $P_t$, knowledge base $K$, and scoring function $f$. A supervisor agent monitors for stagnation and intervenes to steer the exploration.*

The agent operates in a continuous loop. For each variation step, it performs internal iterations of planning, implementation, and evaluation. It can consult prior versions in $P_t$ to analyze why certain optimizations worked or failed. If a candidate fails a correctness check or shows a regression in throughput, the agent diagnoses the error using compiler output or profiling data and revises the code. Only once a candidate meets the performance and correctness criteria is it committed back to the persistent lineage. To prevent the search from getting stuck in local optima, a "Supervisor Agent" monitors the trajectory and provides high-level guidance if it detects stagnation over multiple attempts.

## Optimization of Attention Kernels on NVIDIA Blackwell

The effectiveness of AVO was evaluated by optimizing Multi-Head Attention (MHA) and Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) kernels on the NVIDIA Blackwell B200 GPU. Attention kernels are critical components of Transformer-based AI models, and their performance directly impacts the speed of large language model inference and training. These kernels are typically optimized by hand by hardware experts over several months.

The optimization space for these kernels is vast and involves managing registers, shared memory, and warp-level synchronization. For the AVO agent, the starting point was a seed implementation $x_0$. The agent's goal was to maximize the throughput (measured in TFLOPS) while maintaining strict numerical correctness against a reference implementation.

![MHA Performance Comparison](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.24517v1/x3.png)
*Figure 3: Throughput results for Multi-Head Attention (MHA) on the B200 GPU. AVO-discovered kernels outperform both cuDNN and FlashAttention-4 (FA4) across various sequence lengths.*

The results indicate that AVO successfully discovered micro-architectural optimizations that human experts and existing automated tools had not combined. For causal attention, AVO achieved throughput gains of up to 3.5% over cuDNN and 10.5% over FlashAttention-4. The peak performance reached 1668 TFLOPS at BF16 precision.

## Transferability to Grouped-Query Attention

A significant test of an optimization's value is its generalizability. To assess this, the AVO agent was tasked with adapting the highly optimized MHA kernel it had evolved into a Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) kernel. GQA is a variant of attention used in models like Qwen3 to reduce memory bandwidth requirements by sharing Key and Value heads across multiple Query heads.

![GQA Performance Comparison](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.24517v1/x4.png)
*Figure 4: Throughput for Grouped-Query Attention (GQA). The agent adapted the evolved MHA kernel to GQA in 30 minutes, achieving up to 7% gains over cuDNN.*

The agent completed this adaptation autonomously in approximately 30 minutes. The resulting GQA kernel outperformed both cuDNN and FlashAttention-4 baselines, with gains of up to 7.0% and 9.3% respectively. This demonstrates that the micro-architectural insights discovered by the agent—such as improved instruction scheduling and memory management—were not specific to the MHA layout but represented fundamental hardware-level improvements.

## Analysis of the Evolutionary Trajectory

The optimization process spanned seven days of continuous autonomous evolution. During this time, the agent explored over 500 internal candidate directions, resulting in 40 committed versions that improved the running best performance.

![Causal Evolution Trajectory](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.24517v1/x5.png)
*Figure 5: The evolution trajectory for causal attention shows distinct jumps in performance corresponding to specific micro-architectural breakthroughs.*

The trajectory reveals that performance gains were not linear. Instead, the agent discovered major architectural changes that led to significant jumps in throughput, followed by periods of fine-tuning. Notable milestones included:
- $v8$: Introduction of QK-PV interleaving and bitmask causal masking.
- $v13$: Restructuring the softmax operation into a single pass.
- $v20$: Implementation of branchless accumulator rescaling.
- $v30$: Overlapping the correction and MMA pipelines.
- $v33$: Manual rebalancing of registers across different warp groups.

![Non-Causal Evolution Trajectory](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2603.24517v1/x6.png)
*Figure 6: The evolution trajectory for non-causal attention. While the initial versions showed rapid gains, later versions focused on cycle-level scheduling and resource allocation.*

The agent transitioned from coarse-grained algorithmic changes (like changing how masks are applied) in the early versions ($v1$ to $v20$) to fine-grained, cycle-level tuning (like register allocation) in the later stages ($v21$ to $v40$). This mimicry of a human expert's workflow—starting with the "big ideas" and ending with "squeezing out the last bits of performance"—highlights the sophisticated planning capabilities of the AVO agent.

## Deep Hardware-Level Reasoning: Case Studies

The performance gains achieved by AVO were the result of genuine hardware-level reasoning rather than simple code refactoring. Three specific optimizations found by the agent illustrate this depth:

### Branchless Accumulator Rescaling
In version $v19$ to $v20$, the agent identified a bottleneck in the online softmax algorithm. Traditional implementations use a conditional branch to decide whether to rescale the output accumulator. This branch causes warp divergence and requires heavy synchronization. The agent replaced this with a branchless "speculative" path where the rescale factor is always calculated, and a predicate is used to select the value. This change allowed for a lighter, non-blocking memory fence, resulting in a 8.1% geomean throughput increase for non-causal attention.

### Correction and MMA Pipeline Overlap
Between $v29$ and $v30$, the agent analyzed the dual-stage attention pipeline. It noticed that the "correction" warps (responsible for normalization) were idling while waiting for the second Matrix-Multiply-Accumulate (MMA) stage to finish. The agent restructured the code to allow the correction warps to begin processing the results of the first stage immediately, overlapping their work with the ongoing second MMA stage. This improved throughput by approximately 1.1%.

### Register Rebalancing
In version $v33$, the agent diagnosed a "spilling" issue. Some warps were running out of their allocated registers, causing data to be swapped to slow local memory, while other warps had unused capacity. The agent manually redistributed the register budget—taking 8 registers from the underutilized softmax group and giving them to the critical correction group. This rebalancing (shifting from an allocation of 192/80/48 to 184/88/56) removed the bottleneck and increased performance by 2.1%.

## Significance of the AVO Approach

The development of Agentic Variation Operators signifies a shift in how AI can be used for software engineering. By providing an LLM with the autonomy to act as an agent—complete with tools, memory, and a feedback loop—the search process moves from simple "code generation" to "autonomous engineering."

The ability to surpass expert-tuned libraries in a domain as competitive as GPU kernels suggests that the bottleneck in many optimization tasks is not a lack of hardware knowledge, but the human bandwidth required to iteratively test and refine hundreds of micro-architectural hypotheses. AVO provides a scalable way to automate this process, enabling continuous, 24/7 optimization that can keep pace with the rapid evolution of AI hardware architectures. This framework is not limited to attention kernels and could potentially be applied to a wide range of performance-critical software systems across different hardware platforms.

keyInsights: Elevating LLMs to autonomous "Agentic Variation Operators" enables them to engage in complex, iterative engineering workflows, moving beyond mere candidate generation to genuine code optimization.

Autonomous agents can perform deep hardware-level reasoning, including understanding synchronization, memory ordering, instruction pipeline scheduling, and register allocation, to achieve performance gains.
Continuous, self-directed evolution over extended durations can uncover highly optimized solutions that surpass expert-engineered baselines and are transferable to related computational tasks.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-augmented evolutionary search methods restrict LLMs to single-turn candidate generation, limiting their capacity for complex, iterative optimization workflows.
Optimizing performance-critical computational targets, such as GPU kernels for deep learning, demands intricate, multi-step engineering, which current LLM approaches do not fully support.

Achieving further performance gains on already aggressively optimized implementations like FlashAttention-4 and cuDNN requires deep, sustained, and iterative interaction with hardware specifics and development environments.
results: AVO-discovered multi-head attention (MHA) kernels on NVIDIA Blackwell B200 GPUs achieved up to 3.5% higher throughput than cuDNN and 10.5% higher than FlashAttention-4 for causal attention configurations.
The evolved MHA kernel was autonomously adapted to grouped-query attention (GQA) in approximately 30 minutes, showing up to 7.0% gains over cuDNN and 9.3% over FlashAttention-4 for causal GQA.

The agent identified specific micro-architectural optimizations, such as branchless accumulator rescaling, correction/MMA pipeline overlap, and register rebalancing across warp groups, illustrating its capacity for fine-grained hardware tuning.
solution: Introduce Agentic Variation Operators (AVO), which replace traditional evolutionary variation with an autonomous LLM-powered agent, granting it full agency over the optimization process.

The AVO agent is equipped with planning capabilities, persistent memory, and tool use (e.g., code editing, shell execution, documentation retrieval) to perform self-directed, multi-step optimization loops.

The agent continuously evolves solutions, performing internal edit-evaluate-diagnose cycles, and commits improved versions to a lineage, guided by a self-supervision mechanism to maintain progress over extended periods.
summary.summary: Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) empower large language models to function as autonomous, iterative optimizers in evolutionary search for high-performance code. This framework successfully discovered multi-head attention kernels on NVIDIA Blackwell B200 GPUs that achieved up to 3.5% higher throughput than cuDNN and 10.5% higher than FlashAttention-4, alongside effective transferability to grouped-query attention.

title: AVO: Agentic Variation Operators for Autonomous Evolutionary Search
